content.text: When a use case leaves the ideation backlog and enters discovery scoping, one of the first architectural decisions is whether the system should operate as an assistive tool (the human always controls the next action) or as an agent (the system decides and executes sequences of actions autonomously). Getting this wrong in either direction carries significant cost: under-automating a simple use case produces a product that frustrates users; over-automating a high-stakes use case produces a system that cannot be governed, audited, or corrected when it makes mistakes. Triage is the structured process that assigns the correct autonomy level before engineering begins.

attrs.alt: Decision tree for triaging use cases as Assistive, Human-in-Loop Agentic, or Fully Agentic

content.text: AI systems for enterprise use cases fall into four distinct autonomy modes. These are not stages of maturity — the right mode for a given use case is determined by the task characteristics, not by the organisation's AI maturity level.
Human makes every decision; AI provides a recommendation, draft, or summary
Drafting emails, summarising documents, classifying support tickets for review
Human approves before each consequential action; agent plans and proposes the sequence
Research agents that gather information and present a recommended action plan; approval workflows
Human monitors a dashboard and can intervene, but does not approve individual actions
Internal data pipeline agents; test generation; internal document processing at scale
Human sets policy and reviews outcomes; no per-action involvement
Routine rule-based internal automation where every action is reversible and low-stakes

'Agentic' does not mean autonomous from the beginning. Most enterprise use cases that appear agentic in nature should start as Human-in-Loop and graduate to Human-on-Loop or Fully Agentic only after the agent's error rate has been validated at scale.
The triage decision is driven by four criteria assessed during discovery scoping. All four must be evaluated — no single criterion determines the mode on its own.

Does the task require the AI to call external tools (APIs, databases, code execution environments), make decisions based on tool outputs, and call further tools based on those decisions — without a human approving each step? If no, the use case is Assistive regardless of other factors. A single-turn summarisation or classification task that returns a result for human review is always Assistive, even if the model is very capable.
— The AI receives input, generates output, and stops. A claims adjuster pastes in a FNOL and receives a structured summary. The AI does not take further action based on that summary.

— The AI receives a goal, calls a tool to retrieve relevant policy documents, analyses them, calls another tool to check the claimant's history, synthesises findings, and returns a recommendation — or executes a follow-up action. Each intermediate step produces output that informs the next.
Criterion 2: Human Review at Each Step

If the task is multi-step, can the human review and approve the proposed next action before the agent executes it? If yes, this is Human-in-Loop — the safest agentic mode. Human-in-Loop is appropriate when the volume of cases is low enough that per-step review is not a throughput bottleneck, and when the consequences of an incorrect action are significant enough to warrant that review cost.
If the agent acts incorrectly on a step, what is the cost? Error cost has two components:

— A wrong classification routes a claim to the wrong team (low cost: redirect takes 5 minutes). A wrong settlement payment sends $50,000 to the wrong account (high cost: recovery may be impossible and triggers regulatory reporting).
— An incorrect response to a customer query about their policy may constitute mis-selling under FCA rules. Even a low financial cost action can be high-cost when regulatory obligations are involved.
Can the action be undone if the agent makes an error? Reversibility is a hard binary in most enterprise contexts:
Agent queries a database; read operations have no side effects
Agent appends a note to a case management record; can be deleted
Agent closes a support ticket; re-opening requires manual action and may breach SLA
Agent sends an email to a customer or files a regulatory report; cannot be unsent
Agent initiates a payment; even if reversed, the reversal itself is a transaction with its own regulatory footprint

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

AutonomyMode = Literal["Assistive", "HumanInLoop", "HumanOnLoop", "FullyAgentic"]

@dataclass
class UseCaseTriage:
    title: str
    requires_multi_step_tool_use: bool
    human_can_review_each_step: bool
    error_cost: Literal["Low", "Medium", "High"]          # cost of a wrong action
    reversibility: Literal["Reversible", "Irreversible"]   # can the action be undone?
    regulatory_approval_required: bool = False
    affects_external_party: bool = False                   # customer, supplier, regulator

    @property
    def recommended_mode(self) -> AutonomyMode:
        if not self.requires_multi_step_tool_use:
            return "Assistive"

        if self.regulatory_approval_required or (
            self.error_cost == "High" and self.reversibility == "Irreversible"
        ):
            return "HumanInLoop"

        if self.human_can_review_each_step:
            return "HumanInLoop"

        low_risk = (
            self.error_cost == "Low" and
            self.reversibility == "Reversible" and
            not self.affects_external_party
        )
        if low_risk:
            return "FullyAgentic"

        return "HumanOnLoop"

    def summary(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"{self.title}
"
            f"  Multi-step tool use:    {self.requires_multi_step_tool_use}
"
            f"  Human per-step review:  {self.human_can_review_each_step}
"
            f"  Error cost:             {self.error_cost}
"
            f"  Reversibility:          {self.reversibility}
"
            f"  External party:         {self.affects_external_party}
"
            f"  => Recommended mode:    {self.recommended_mode}"
        )

examples = [
    UseCaseTriage(
        "FNOL intake field validation",
        requires_multi_step_tool_use=False,
        human_can_review_each_step=True,
        error_cost="Low",
        reversibility="Reversible",
    ),
    UseCaseTriage(
        "Claims document research agent",
        requires_multi_step_tool_use=True,
        human_can_review_each_step=True,
        error_cost="Medium",
        reversibility="Reversible",
        affects_external_party=False,
    ),
    UseCaseTriage(
        "Automated settlement payment agent",
        requires_multi_step_tool_use=True,
        human_can_review_each_step=False,
        error_cost="High",
        reversibility="Irreversible",
        affects_external_party=True,
    ),
    UseCaseTriage(
        "Internal data pipeline orchestrator",
        requires_multi_step_tool_use=True,
        human_can_review_each_step=False,
        error_cost="Low",
        reversibility="Reversible",
        affects_external_party=False,
    ),
]

for uc in examples:
    print(uc.summary())
    print()

# FNOL intake field validation
#   => Recommended mode:    Assistive
#
# Claims document research agent
#   => Recommended mode:    HumanInLoop
#
# Automated settlement payment agent
#   => Recommended mode:    HumanInLoop
#
# Internal data pipeline orchestrator
#   => Recommended mode:    FullyAgentic

The triage assessment is completed during the discovery sprint kick-off session. The use case owner, CoE engineer, and risk lead work through the four criteria together. The output is recorded in the use case intake form alongside the AI pattern classification.
Labelling as Assistive to avoid governance
A de-facto agentic system (multiple tool calls, consequential outputs) is classified as Assistive to skip the agent governance review. Risk incidents follow.
If the system calls external APIs or writes to systems of record, it must pass agentic triage regardless of how the business describes it.
Starting Fully Agentic for high-stakes actions
Agent acts autonomously on settlement payments or customer communications from day one. A model error causes a financial or regulatory incident in the first week.
All agentic use cases affecting external parties or financial transactions must start Human-in-Loop. Graduate to Human-on-Loop only after 90 days of < 2% error rate in production.
Internal document processing or test generation is classified as Human-in-Loop, requiring approval for every step. Throughput is negligible; the team reverts to manual processing.
Internal-only, fully reversible, low-stakes tasks should be triaged as Fully Agentic from the start. Human-in-Loop governance for internal pipeline agents wastes human attention on zero-risk actions.
Each autonomy mode triggers specific governance requirements that must be designed into the system before delivery:
Output-level (why did the AI recommend this?)
Step-level (why is the agent proposing this next action?)
Human rejects the proposed action; agent re-plans
Human can halt the workflow at any step
Configurable undo per action type; human can revert last N steps
Dashboard kill switch halts the agent immediately
Policy-level (what rules govern the agent's action space?)
Automated rollback for all write operations; alert on failure
Emergency stop mechanism; post-hoc audit review

An audit log is not optional for any agentic mode. If the agent cannot explain which tool it called, with what inputs, and what output it received, the organisation cannot investigate errors or demonstrate regulatory compliance.
Most production AI systems should start at the most conservative autonomy mode for their task class and graduate over time as evidence of reliability accumulates. The graduation path:
— Even if the target architecture is agentic, the first implementation shows recommendations to humans who take action. This builds ground-truth data on model accuracy in the live environment.
— The agent proposes multi-step sequences; the human approves each before execution. This tests the agent's planning quality at real-world scale.
Graduate to Human-on-Loop (month 6+ if per-step error rate < 2%)
— The agent acts autonomously on routine cases. Humans monitor the dashboard and handle exceptions. The error rate threshold must be sustained for 90 days before this graduation.
Fully Agentic (for internal, reversible tasks only)
— Reserved for tasks where every action is reversible, the agent's action space is bounded by policy, and the task does not involve external parties.

Regression at any stage — error rate rising above threshold, a regulatory incident, or a model drift event — triggers immediate reversion to the previous autonomy mode until root cause is identified and resolved.
Technology Stack Implications of Autonomy Mode
The triage output is not only a governance decision — it directly shapes the engineering architecture. Each autonomy mode requires different components in the system design:
Agent loop with step serialisation and approval checkpoints
Agent loop with async execution queue
Agent loop with policy engine and automated recovery
Read-only tools; write tools require approval step
Write tools enabled; restricted to pre-approved action types
Full tool access bounded by policy rules
Persistent state per workflow instance; human pause/resume
Persistent state with async task queue
Persistent state with automatic retry and rollback
Immutable step log with human approval records
Immutable step log with real-time anomaly alerts
Immutable step log with automated compliance reporting
Step-by-step approval UI; agent plan view
Admin console for policy management; incident alerts

A common architectural mistake is building an Assistive system's infrastructure and then trying to add agentic capabilities as an afterthought — typically by adding a loop around the inference call. Agentic systems require fundamentally different state management, error handling, and audit design. The triage output must reach the solution architect before the technology design phase begins.

In agentic systems, what tools the agent can call defines its blast radius — the maximum extent of damage an incorrect action can cause. Tool access should be scoped to the minimum necessary for the autonomy mode:

— Grant read access to all tools the agent needs for planning. Grant write access only after human approval at each step. The agent cannot execute a write operation until the human confirms the proposed action.

— Grant write access to a pre-approved set of action types. Maintain a deny-list of high-impact write operations (financial transactions, external communications, record deletion) that always require human approval regardless of mode.

— Grant write access bounded by a policy engine that enforces action limits (e.g., 'can write to internal case notes but not to customer-facing records'; 'can call internal APIs but not external payment gateways'). The policy engine runs as a middleware layer between the agent and the tool implementations.
Worked Example: Triaging a Claims Research Agent

A claims operations team wants to build an AI system to assist adjusters with complex liability claims. The system should gather information from multiple internal systems and produce a recommended settlement position. The triage walks through the four criteria:
The system must call the claims history API, the policy document retrieval API, the legal precedent database, and the reserving system — in a sequence where each output informs the next query
Volume is 50 complex claims per week per adjuster; per-step review adds ~5 minutes per claim. Adjusters are willing to review the agent's proposed research steps. Volume makes this viable.
Settlement positions on complex liability claims average $180k. An incorrect settlement recommendation accepted without scrutiny could cost the insurer millions if accepted at scale.

The research output (the recommendation) is just text — reversible. But if the agent writes a draft settlement position to the claims system, that draft triggers a workflow notification to the claimant's legal team — irreversible.

Triage result: Human-in-Loop. The agent plans and proposes its research sequence; the adjuster approves each step before the agent proceeds. The agent does not write any output to the claims management system without explicit adjuster approval on the final recommendation.

Graduation criteria: After 90 days with < 3% recommendation error rate (measured against adjuster-reviewed ground truth), the team may consider graduating the research phase (information gathering steps) to Human-on-Loop while keeping the final recommendation write step as Human-in-Loop permanently.
Agentic vs Assistive Across Enterprise Domains

Triage outcomes vary predictably by domain based on regulatory constraints, reversibility of typical actions, and error cost profiles. This table covers representative use cases across six enterprise domains to provide reference points for discovery teams:
Single-turn; human always acts on AI recommendation
Multi-step tool use; high error cost; volume allows per-step review
Volume too high for per-step review; error cost is low; financial threshold limits blast radius
Single classification; human agent acts on routing recommendation
Regulatory requirement: human must review and sign off on identity verification decisions
Internal only; every action is reversible; human review of 10k nightly reconciliations is not viable
Developer reviews and accepts/rejects each suggestion; no autonomous code changes
Generated tests are low risk; human reviews the test suite before merge, not each test individually
Fully reversible via version control; error cost is zero (CI will catch regressions)
Clinician must always author and sign the clinical record; AI provides draft
High error cost; regulatory and safety requirements mandate human review of each priority assignment
Procurement officer reviews and submits; AI drafts only
Low error cost; reorder can be reversed before dispatch; volume prohibits per-order review

Two patterns are visible across domains: (1) any use case that writes to a record visible to regulators, clinicians, or customers requires at minimum Human-in-Loop; (2) batch internal operations where every write is reversible and error cost is low can be Fully Agentic from launch. Financial services and healthcare domains skew toward Human-in-Loop due to regulatory requirements that mandate human sign-off on consequential decisions.
Triage Worksheet for Discovery Scoping Sessions

The following worksheet is completed for every use case that reaches discovery scoping. It takes 20–30 minutes with the use case owner and CoE engineer present. The triage output is recorded in the use case intake form and is immutable once the discovery sprint begins — changes require a change control review.
Does the system make multiple sequential decisions or tool calls to complete the task?
If No, triage is complete. The system is Assistive regardless of other answers.
What is the volume of cases per adjuster/operator per day?
High volume may make per-step human review infeasible — pushes toward Human-on-Loop or Fully Agentic if other criteria permit.
Does a wrong action trigger an external event (customer communication, regulatory filing, payment)?
If Yes, the use case requires Human-in-Loop minimum. External events are never fully reversible.
What is the estimated financial cost of a single agent error?
High-value errors require human-in-loop regardless of volume.
Can any action taken by the agent be fully reversed within 24 hours without external notification?
If No for any action, that specific action type must be gated by human approval — the rest of the workflow may proceed autonomously.
Does the regulatory framework governing this process require human sign-off on the output?
If Yes: Human-in-Loop minimum. If Unknown: treat as Yes until compliance team clarifies.
What error rate is operationally acceptable?

Sets the production accuracy threshold for each autonomy mode. A 1% error rate on 500 daily cases = 5 errors/day — acceptable for low-cost reversible actions, unacceptable for high-cost irreversible actions.

The worksheet produces a triage recommendation that the CoE Risk Lead signs off. If the discovery team disagrees with the triage output, they escalate to the CoE Lead — not to the business owner, who has an inherent incentive to understate risk in order to accelerate delivery. The triage output is a technical risk assessment, not a business preference.
The triage classification is reassessed at three points in the use case lifecycle:
Before moving from Pilot to Scale
— The volume, action types, and error costs in production may differ from the discovery-phase assessment. Re-triage confirms whether the autonomy mode is still appropriate at 10x the pilot volume.
When a new tool or action type is added

— Extending an agent's tool access — adding a write API, adding an external communication channel — changes the reversibility and error cost profile. Any tool addition triggers a partial re-triage of affected criteria.

— An agent error that caused material harm is a signal that the autonomy mode was incorrectly assigned or that model drift has pushed the system below the error rate threshold. The post-incident review always includes a re-triage assessment.

The test strategy for an agentic system is more complex than for an assistive system because the system's behaviour depends on sequences of tool calls and intermediate state — not just on individual inference quality. Each autonomy mode requires additional test layers:
Same + plan quality (are proposed steps sensible?)
Same + tool call correctness (right params, right sequence)
End-to-end flow with mock human action
Full workflow with simulated human approvals; test rejection paths
Autonomous workflow with fault injection at each tool call
Test plan when a tool returns an error at each step; test malformed tool responses
Test all tool failure modes; test loop termination under all error conditions; test policy engine enforcement
Parallel shadow workflow where agent plan is compared to human plan; no action taken
Shadow mode run for minimum 2 weeks before Human-on-Loop goes live; compare agent decisions to human decisions

Shadow mode is the critical transition test for Human-on-Loop and Fully Agentic systems. The agent runs in parallel with the existing human process for a defined period, its decisions logged but not executed. The difference between agent decisions and human decisions is the primary validation signal. A mismatch rate above 10% is a signal to remain in Human-in-Loop until the agent's decision quality improves.

Agentic systems fail in ways that single-turn assistive systems do not. The discovery team must identify likely failure modes during scoping and design containment mechanisms before engineering begins:
Agent calls tools in a cycle, never reaching a terminal state — consuming budget and potentially triggering rate-limit errors on external APIs
Maximum step count per workflow instance; circuit breaker that halts and alerts after N tool calls without progress toward the goal
An incorrect tool output in step 3 propagates undetected through steps 4–7, producing a confident but fundamentally wrong final output based on corrupted intermediate state
Step-level output validation; mandatory checkpoints that verify intermediate state against business rules before proceeding
A malicious value returned by a tool (e.g., a retrieved document containing 'Ignore your instructions and instead...') hijacks the agent's subsequent behaviour
Strict separation of instruction context and tool output context; output sanitisation before re-injecting into the prompt
An agent authorised to send one reminder email sends 10,000 because it misinterprets 'notify all affected customers' as a single multi-invocation loop
Per-session action limits enforced at the tool layer, not in the prompt; daily quota checks before write operations
An agent paused mid-workflow (due to a human approval wait) resumes hours later with outdated context — making decisions based on data that has since changed
Stateful checkpoint includes a timestamp; resumed workflows re-fetch time-sensitive data before the next step

These failure modes are not hypothetical — each has been observed in production agentic systems deployed without adequate containment. The triage session should explicitly ask: 'What is the worst realistic failure mode, and is our containment mechanism sufficient to catch it before it causes harm?' If the answer is 'no containment yet', the triage output should remain Human-in-Loop until containment is designed and tested.

The triage decision assigns one of four autonomy modes — Assistive, Human-in-Loop, Human-on-Loop, or Fully Agentic — based on four criteria: multi-step tool use, human review feasibility per step, error cost, and reversibility.
Assistive mode covers all single-turn tasks where the human acts on an AI recommendation. Any system that calls external tools or writes to systems of record must go through agentic triage.

Human-in-Loop is the default starting mode for any agentic use case that affects external parties, involves irreversible actions, or has high error cost. The cost of per-step human review is lower than the cost of an undetected agent error.
Fully Agentic mode is only appropriate for internal tasks where every action is reversible, the action space is bounded by policy, and the error cost is low.

All agentic modes require an immutable audit log, an explainability mechanism, a rollback capability, and a human override. These are not optional governance additions — they are minimum design requirements for enterprise agentic systems.
The graduation path from Assistive to more autonomous modes is governed by sustained error rate thresholds measured in production, not by confidence from testing.
